danger_signals.meaning: Instant death hazard; environmental threat requiring platforming skill
Impassable hazard; thermal danger environment state
Impassable due to protagonist inability to swim; drowning hazard
Triggered hazards requiring detection and avoidance
Combat threat; must be defeated with club weapon

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible stone items from defeated enemies or pickups
safety_signals.meaning: Defense state; stones loaded into club provide single-hit protection
Safe zone for commerce; bone currency converts to upgrades and spells

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Paradise Island, Monster Island, Terror Island, Chaos Island progression

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Island-specific enemy types (monster island fauna, chaos creatures)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Presence indicates hostile world-state per island

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bone collectibles scattered throughout levels as quest objective
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must hunt and collect bones to purchase items; drives exploration and combat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental theming escalates from safe/peaceful to hostile/dangerous across four distinct island locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Difficulty increases; enemy density and hazard complexity escalates with island tier

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Club weapon held by protagonist; stones loaded into club (up to three visible)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can engage enemies; must strike enemies instead of jumping on them (core mechanic)
Single hit causes death; must avoid all contact with enemies and hazards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Purchased spells and upgrades (visual implementation not detailed in sources)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enhanced movement or combat capabilities purchased from shops using bone currency

environmental_storytelling.example: Paradise Island → Monster Island → Terror Island → Chaos Island; escalating threat narrative through location names
Lava/water barriers increase in frequency and complexity across islands, signaling escalating danger world-state
Bone collectibles embedded in dangerous areas, creating risk-reward tension and establishing world as hostile/resource-limited
Island-specific enemy fauna (dinosaur references in Wikipedia) establish environmental identity and prehistoric setting

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can engage enemies by striking; stones discharge as projectiles on impact
Vulnerable to single hit death; must avoid all enemies and hazards; forced to evade
Patrol/attack patterns; killed by club strikes; drop bones on defeat
Static; facilitate bone-to-item trades; enable progression through ability upgrades

notes: Big Nose Freaks Out (1992) is a side-scrolling platformer with strong environmental hazard communication. The game uses island progression as primary world-state indicator, with visual theming escalating from 'Paradise' to 'Chaos.' Danger is communicated through void gaps, animated lava/water barriers, and enemy presence. Safety is signaled through stone acquisition (defensive buff), solid terrain, and shop zones. The club-based combat system (striking enemies rather than jumping on them) is the core mechanic differentiating this from standard platformers. Bone collection drives exploration and establishes resource scarcity as core world narrative. Limited source material; Wikipedia article notes game is about dinosaurs and set in prehistory, adding thematic context to hazard/creature design. TCRF source minimal (deletion candidate). Tier T3 qualitative data based on gameplay descriptions and design patterns.
